speaker: interviewer
text: --for interview with Abraham--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jachzel.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jachzel. OK, whenever you are ready, you go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jachzel, you-- we started talking about the ghetto, how your father and mother and two sisters and you went back and decided to live at the ghetto, in Cracow, with the other Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to please describe for us how the room that you lived in looked, how many people were there in that one room or two rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. Well, when we came back, when we went to the ghetto, we had-- we-- all we had is our beddings, you know. So you know, the bedding wasn't like here, you know, with a feather bed, the cushions. Pots and pans, that's what we had, you know, all this stuff and the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and this, when we  went to ghetto, what are we going to do here? You know, just Father said, let's set up a shop. So we got-- we got some machines. And we set up a shop in the ghetto. So what do we do? We cannot-- there's no horses. And we can't do skis. I remember, we started doing those end pieces for the shoes, you know, the-- you know, to save this, you know? Right, like taps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tops, yeah, we did tops. Then we still had the orders from-- there was a [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a big company what made asbestos roofs. And we did the clips. You know, it's-- it's like a nail. After you push through the hole, you bend it over. And so they don't-- they hold together with this one copper nail. And the copper nail-- there was a nail, copper nail, with a flat piece around three-quarter inch copper, like altering material. And it has to be connected.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we start doing this to fulfill the orders. And that's-- new money came in. Prices went up. We couldn't get the copper. They supplied the copper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then was-- then we got another-- not a wet press. We bought a press, over there, a power press, electric  one. We had hand presses, which were you don't use them in the United States. But they're quite-- for a secondary operation, they may be-- they're much better than the power presses. And so if you do it with your hand, and it caught it here, you're not going to chop your fingers off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life in the ghetto went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- well, we had to set-- set up something. You had to make a living. So we set up a-- a-- a shop. As a matter of fact, we had a couple, from our workers, Polish guys, come in to, you know, to work in the ghetto. You know, they came for a whole week, slept over, and they went home on Friday. So it was the-- a reverse. They worked till they say, oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They saw what's going on. They say, no more. You know? They stopped-- they stopped coming. And while they were coming in, they were bringing food and stuff like that. But since we were doing for outside work, we had-- everybody had a Kennkarte, you know? It was a yellow card with your picture. And it was monthly stamped. So I remember, I used to go to go and get it stamped in-- you know, by the SS, you know, the Totenkopf Regiment, whatever it is-- for me, for my father, and for the two Polacks who used to come in. They were the reverse cards, you know? And even if they didn't work, I keep on going this way. It was a important thing. You know, it was a still important business, even we did a-- a minimum, you know? So I had-- first of all, I had the freedom to go out, you know? But I shouldn't go with-- with the the arm band. The yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a yellow star. It was white armband with a-- a blue star, you know? [NON-ENGLISH] Magen David [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody wore that kind, anyone who was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you wear-- you wear this all the time in ghetto. But when you went out from ghetto, you were lucky enough to have the passport, you know, on the Kennkarte, which I had, which was-- I was abusing. My father had to hide it from me, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But all the-- the-- everybody-- I want to ask you about this, the blue star.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody who was in the Cracow ghetto, child, women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the ghetto. This started--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it start?

speaker: interviewee
text: --still when we were in the Jewish quarters, you know, in Cracow, in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the army came in is that when it started?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was later on, later on. There was-- you know, every time was something else. There was a Jewish Kehilla, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is like-- the Jews have to-- even before the war, we pay taxes.

speaker: interviewer
text: An organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not organization. This was-- like the churches had their place where they were getting money. And the Jewish rabbis got paid in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And there-- there had to be a [NON-ENGLISH]. There had to be a-- a mohel. You know, which my grandfather was a mohel, which had to get paid, but why? But people, you know, so [NON-ENGLISH]. But, you know, you couldn't-- those-- those people couldn't live from just mitzvahs. You know, if somebody-- you know, you-- you had a [NON-ENGLISH], you'd give him 5 zloty, 10 zloty. You give something.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now people couldn't do it, or they didn't want to do it. So it-- it wouldn't be any mohels, if they-- they had to pay the difference, you know? The Kehilla paid. Let's say, if my uncle was a rosh yeshiva, you know, the city, over there, they didn't have this. This was a city job, even it was-- it-- it was like-- like-- even if it wasn't a church, you know, and it was a Jewish thing. But it-- it was a-- if it was a city job. But the Kehilla didn't pay-- only paid for like the ritual slaughterer--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --paid for poor Jews who had no place--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Kehilla had-- had a lot. First of all was a-- a Jewish hospital. And, you know, tell a Jew that you have to pay. You know, it's not a easy thing, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: I just got a-- they raised my-- my wife's insurance. All right, I'm already on Medicare. You know? And I'm looking at the amount. I'm getting-- the limit is $5,000-- $203 a month. So it's already $2,400, $2,450-- $1,000 deductible. So I'm paying $3,500 to be $5,000 insured. I mean it's ridiculous. So all right. We're going back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going back, because we're supposed to talk about something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me, was there a-- a physical barrier in this Cracow ghetto? Was there a wall? Was there a fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In-- in Cracow ghetto, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ghetto, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but Cracow quarters, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the ghetto. We're--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto, yeah, well, sure. There was a front-- a cement gate, you know, where you can go through with a horse and buggy or a-- or a truck. There were like one-- in Cracow ghetto, I only remember two entrances. You know? Two entrances would be big enough to-- to get through with the truck. And I think there was one-- one more, which you can you just get in and out, you know, not a entrance to get in with the-- with the commerce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, who-- were there guards at the gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Germans or Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- usually, there was-- there was two. There was Polish guards. And there was Jewish guards. You know, right away, when the-- when the ghetto started, there was a Jewish police, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, and there was a German. But I don't think the German was all the time, you know? When something was going on, there were 10 of them, on each thing and all around when they-- when they were sending out or killing or-- or whatever they did, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the normal, like in the daytime, middle of the week-- usually, all those things happen on weekends.

speaker: interviewer
text: All what things?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when they were taking to Treblinka or to wherever it is and killing people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: --most of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about when that started and how you saw it. How did you know they were killing people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the minute, you know, when I saw that-- And still, the ghetto, OK, I could get out. And once I was a little far out, I ripped it off. I went to the movies. I said to hell with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ripped the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, what I'm not supposed to do. But you-- you're supposed to be dehumanized. You-- you're not supposed to have a telephone. You cannot ride the street car. You cannot use the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you're not supposed to go to the movies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] None of-- no, no, none of the entertainments. But the circus was still in town. And I had to go see it or whatever it is, you know? I wouldn't miss it. I don't want to be a rabbi. I want to be the announcer in the circus. The-- this was-- this was the type of kid I was. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you notice that-- that people were starting to be killed? Did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. This is this-- when I start-- when I-- when I start and you see that, then my head went-- then-- then-- then my father didn't know how to hold me anymore. I wanted to get out from that ghetto, so badly, and stay out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you see it and how did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I saw it, this was still around 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw people being killed in front of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't kill you in front of you. They took you out. And-- and you never came back, you understand? They put them on the train and-- and disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, don't forget, every time, you know, they started killing, I mean, people are not completely lambs. You know, the people start yelling, start pushing and stuff, you know? I mean, there was-- and-- there was enough free men.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they give the job to the Jewish police. You know, they said, listen, we got to get rid of 10,000 people. There's too many people around here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Jewish police made up lists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had to deliver. And you know, in a way, didn't know what to do. One time, they came to pick up my sister. And she was hidden in a-- how you call it, [NON-ENGLISH] you know, How would you call here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like an armoire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like I got over there, to put, like a dresser, but it was open to hang. Here, you don't hang here you-- your coats and suits in that thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's an armoire. It's like a big closet that you open.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was hidden there. And the guys knew is it's a [NON-ENGLISH] and now I talk to too much. You know what I'm saying? [NON-ENGLISH]!

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish police said to your father, we came to get your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they-- no, and I-- But they knew him, you know? They said, well, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father said she ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you know? OK. You know. And the kid, you know, she is now-- I was talking with her a couple hours ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and they left. But they didn't want it, you know? The Jewish police wasn't-- I mean, there-- they wasn't Gestapo. They had to do dirty tricks, dirty things. And I felt bad for them, you know, what they have to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were being given orders by the German police?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had got the German by the orders. And the funny thing is, most of the time, the-- the Kehilla, you know, the-- they were trying to get college boys or something, so to have a little heart, so they don't do damage, you know, to the-- to the right-- well, right people, one way. Instead of the Jews, you know, they had to get rid of the other ones. So they-- so they covered up, you know, a little bit, for the ones they knew, for their friends or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- did-- when you-- when people didn't come back, how do you know they were dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that it's dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you come--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- she had a-- there-- there were-- once they put them on a-- on a-- on a train, and they put-- they wired the windows, and it's a cattle train, you know? And they locked the doors. And they only leave like for-- for breathing, like four inches, you know? That is a-- well, oh. I think the trains are built like this, you know, so you can have a partial opening and still locked in. And some of them came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this train? Did you ever see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I see the train, when I was in ghetto, later on, you know, this was my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: To put in-- to nail in the windows. You know? So when we nailed in the windows. And we threw in a pair of pliers to every wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: No kidding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so in case they can save themself or they want to save themselves, go ahead and do it. You threw in a hammer. You came back. And you had no tools.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you also risked your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what can we do? What do we do? What do we do? So we had another piece of iron you hit it with. You had the nails and everything. And that's a-- this was the locksmith's job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened after--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after your sister was found-- was not found. How did-- how did life continue then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It continued. They needed a woman to work at the airport. It wasn't an airport like here. There was airport for the-- for the soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were-- how many airplanes the Germans had at that time? I mean there wasn't-- I mean Polacks didn't have any. They got four bombers from England, you know? They were good for-- for one day. Were the actions-- were there actions in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was the type of actions there was. You know, when they-- when they needed, they took 1,000 people, 2,000 people. And they took them-- that time, that did not-- They killed. But, you know, you stay home. You don't want to look. You don't go look for it. You heard, OK, that four people got killed, six people. This, they took to Montelupich, this there.

speaker: interviewer
text: They start-- we start hearing about Auschwitz. They were giving soap, who was very, you know, like Lava soap, you know? Very rough.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was kind of rough. And they would say, they're going to make soap from you, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was saying that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guys between themselves, the kids, you know? We're going-- we-- we're going to become soap, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Young boys were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you know? It's-- you-- you-- you talk. You're kids. You don't-- I mean, reality is reality. It start hitting. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the deportations begin from the Cracow ghetto? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, it's all dated. I don't keep a-- a-- a diary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how the people were assembled for the deportations? How they were assembled? No. But they-- they went house by house, with a list, from beginning. And at the end, when they took, you know, three-quarters of the people, when my mother went, and my aunt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I never mentioned this. My mother's sister-- it was her stupidity, but she got pregnant. And she had a baby. And this was the date of the bris.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that was a week after-- the-- the baby was born on Saturday. The bris was on Saturday. And on the same-- and next week, Saturday, it was the big thing, you know? So I remember-- The big what? The big?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where they took us all to the concentration camp, to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: The entire Cracow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole camp. So I remember, there was Berta, my-- my aunt. And she was a gorgeous, beautiful-- my young-- my youngest aunt and with-- going with the baby. And she didn't know what it-- you know, and everything, the tumult and everything. And she's got a seven-day-- a-- a two-week's old baby, you know, seven days after the bris, you know, wrapped up in a newspaper. And it was holding, getting it all. Somebody told me that a woman came to-- [PHONE RINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, gosh, that's the other telephone. Somebody told you what? [PHONE RINGING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody-- Close the door. When I came to-- to the ghetto, she was there. And [NON-ENGLISH], you know, her husband, was there, which he was an accountant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and we knew it, the way it was. So I said, what happened to the baby? What happened? Some woman, could have been my mother, took the baby from her and said, listen, take a rest, you know? I'll hold-- but the baby was crying. So they said, take a rest. I'll hold the baby for you, you know, and everything. Your mother said this to her sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if it was my mother. But the way I heard, there was a woman came in and said, Berta, take a rest. I'll hold the baby for you. You know, the baby was crying. It was awful. It was a little boy. And then I heard that one of the Lithuanian soldiers grabbed the baby, you know, crying baby from her and hit it against the wall, smash-- smashed it against the wall. [SIGHS] Anyway-- or she saw it or she didn't see it. When she came to-- when we came back to-- took us to concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't. It was an Arbeitslager Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, arbeitslager is a work camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a work camp. But becomes-- later, it became concentration camp. And the same one, there was no change. They put more wires, more electric wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arbeitslager was no picnic, was  it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not. It was the same thing. And then, the first day, they brought in all the dead ones. And there was 15,000. They brought them to bury. You see, they're sent out. Then the rest didn't-- couldn't assemble. They didn't have enough trains, so whatever it is, they shot them on the spot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? We came in. When was it? Monday? Right the next day, they're taking people to bury the people. They don't grab me. Oh, my father said, oh, no. He know what I'd never make it, you know? Where was your mother and your two sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, they took away. But that sister, Berta, what they took away the baby and smashed against the wall, came in, and she made it. She came into the concentration-- to the arbeitslager with us. But I remember like now. She was sitting like this. She had a nervous breakdown like this. Her husband was just sitting next to her and every-- And the woman, over there, they knew that she won't eat. They used to give her, spoon by spoon. She-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SOBBING] And I went to see her, you know? She never recognized me. The funny thing is they did the work for her. She had to do work on a sewing machine. You know, [SIGHS] we are still Jews, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you know what? When I used to-- you know, it took more than a-- a year, I used to go there, you know? And she'd never recognize me, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she went-- she-- she was working. And she didn't know where to stop, once they taught her how to do it, you know, on the sewing machine. She didn't know. The last thing I heard, when they took out, from Plaszow, she went to Stutthof, which wasn't such a bad concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And something happened that-- they-- they wanted to save-- who-- I don't know if it was a German or a working camp. They wanted to save, from Stutthof, some people and bring them to Sweden. You know, it was something like Schindler, whatever, you know, somebody? And there was a bombing. And they start yelling, those women and everything. The ones that kept quiet, some of them made it. But the ones that they start yelling, the people had to choke them, you know? Whatever, this-- and that's where she-- that-- this was the end of my Aunt Berta. Berta?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened to your mother and your little sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my sister, all those people went, I think, to Treblinka at that time, whatever it was. And after the war, you know, Rav Lewertow was a friend of he was a-- the-- the baker rabbi of Cracow. This is dynasty. This is no-- once they have it, this is father to son and to grand-- you know, that's how it goes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the Hasidic dynasty?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Cracow dynasty. This has completely got nothing to do with Hasidic. I think this is the law. That's how they're set up in the City of Cracow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [SIGHS] they set up it. They made the first day of Rosh Chodesh Kislev, but Rosh Chodesh Kislev can be two days, certain years, right? Maybe around November, October time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I just have a card. Now, it's out. So he say look, you're sad, you give 50 years. 49, they say, 1 year you live for the Jew. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 50 years is gone. So I don't keep anymore. But I get-- I light a candle for my-- for my-- Do you want to tell us your mother and your sister's name, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother-- [CRYING] Golda Klainberger from Rahm. And my sister name was Hadassah, Hodeh, Hodeh Jachzel. She was the youngest. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And that was about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Who told you about your mother and your sister's fate? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: A postcard came in. Somehow, my mother had always-- you know, she was very good in correspondence and stuff like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or she threw out the postcard on her way, you know? And-- or she would give it to somebody who was running behind and through the windows, you know, somehow, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a postcard that the-- that we are quite comfortable. And-- [SIGHS] that's-- that's about this. And take care of yourself. And don't stick together. And that was the setup. That each one is going to move, separately, so maybe some of us survive. If we would all go together, you know, we might not survive. But one is going to stick up for the other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the postcard was from Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: That postcard came in to the ghetto. And we got the-- all we got is the postcard. Matter of fact, after the war, my-- you see, I didn't get me my mother's picture. [SIGHS] [SOBS] After the war, all I got-- and when I went to the committee in Cracow, they had the Kennkarte, you know, from my mother, what she threw out, through the window, with her-- with her picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr. Jachzel, tell me how you-- how you survived. What gave you the strength and the will to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: The will to survive wasn't in my father. You see, I looked at those wires. I look at his-- I wanted the end of it. I figured out I-- I was figuring, maybe, I can take somebody with me, you know? But I I was down, very much down. I didn't know what's going to happen. Matter of fact, when they were taken to Mauthausen-- this was just before when we went to Schindler-- We were four guys hidden, not far from a-- under a German barrack. We figure, hey, under our barracks, they're going to look. I mean, this was a German barrack, what they called the [NON-ENGLISH] magazine, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had all the loot stored over there, all the clothes where there would still-- start to give it to us, you know? And they were still ripping apart. Maybe, they found a piece of gold here and it was a few dollars or English pounds or whatever it is. So we were hidden under there. But-- and then they sent out everybody. We-- there was only like 300-- no. From 40,000, there was left maybe 1,500 people-- From 40,000 Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Plaszow. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Plaszow. How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was-- Wait a minute. There was more Polacks already than Jews. But the Pole-- the Polish camp was inside our camp. They had it worse, you know? They-- you know, we-- we were all around them, you know? There was still a Polish camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, well, I got kind of hungry. You know, we didn't have food to store. There is no food to store, you know? And I-- I remember, I went to the latrine. And I start looking around. And all I see is the Polish uniforms. And I don't see any Jews, you know? I said, well, what happened, you know? What's going to be? I'm going to be here with just the Polack. But then, I saw a guy, you know, who work-- he had-- he-- And I say, well, what's going on? So he says, don't worry. Your father is here. So what happened? I went over to my father. My father said, [NON-ENGLISH] you know? So what-- what do you do? He say, well, I saw ghetto transport. And I was on a [NON-ENGLISH]. So he go, you know? Dad, don't rush.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just don't rush. [NON-ENGLISH] And this was already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just about four weeks before we went with Schindler. You see that only the people who left-- who were left over, most of it woman-- there were more.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were mostly the people who they hold back so they can set up a Schindler camp, you know? Now, tell me, this was in the ghetto? Oh, this was in Plaszow, still?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow in concentration camp. This was Plaszow concentration. And what happened? I said, [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: They were looking for me. [NON-ENGLISH] Ginter. And Ginter was the man who-- who tried to get the mechanics. He knew it, you know. How is he going to set up a shop, a factory, with doctors and lawyers and-- and professionals and millionaires and whatever, you know, people who never worked in their life, you know? As he had. So he said, Shloyme, [NON-ENGLISH] OK, so he said, Shloyme, to your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where is your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an electrical engineer. And we used to give him jobs. You know, So he-- he knew it, you know? I mean he is in United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Matter of fact, I got once a job from Sweden, cash registers, you know? And-- and-- and I see a guy. He looks like his son, you know? And I know his son was taken out from-- from concentration camp, brought in to Polacks. And-- and-- and I ask him, you know, are you-- is your father this Gunther, you know, he was from Cracow? He said, I don't know what you're talking about. He didn't want to-- So I said, all right, I'm sorry, you know? But I could have swear it, it was him. You know, he was the main engineer. And that-- but he was so good to me. I had the pick of the crab, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like there was a-- a-- a computerized cash register that's at least 400 pounds. He give me 60. All right, and I reply. He say, OK, you know, it's all right, you know. But the dies are too much, and the piece part and so on. But the dies were practically for nothing. I add up $0.02 to every piece, you know, and give him for nothing the dies. I got--
